messages.content: Which of the following statements about defining and using overloaded constructors are true?

In order to determine which of the following statements about defining and using overloaded constructors are true, we need to analyze each statement individually:

1. Overloaded constructors have the same name but different numbers or types of parameters.

This statement is true. Overloaded constructors are defined using the same name as the class, but they differ in the number or types of parameters they accept.

2. Overloaded constructors are used to create multiple objects of the same class with different initial values.

This statement is true. Overloaded constructors allow the creation of objects with different initial states by providing different sets of parameters during instantiation.

3. Overloaded constructors can have a return type other than void.

This statement is false. Constructors, by definition, do not have a return type. They are automatically called when an object is created and their purpose is to initialize the object.

4. Overloaded constructors can have the same number of parameters but different types.

This statement is true. Overloaded constructors can have the same number of parameters, but as long as the types are different, they are considered distinct constructors.

5. Overloaded constructors cannot call one another.

This statement is false. Overloaded constructors can call each other using the "this()" keyword. This allows for code reuse and avoids redundancy when certain parameters have the same default values.

In conclusion, statements 1, 2, 4, and 5 are true, while statement 3 is false.

Which of the following statements about defining and using overloaded constructors are true?
a_Overloaded constructors should be defined using different lists of parameters.
b_Overloaded constructors can only be defined by changing the access modifier.
c_Overloaded constructors can have different access modifiers.
d_A constructor can call another constructor using the keyword this.
e_A constructor can call another constructor using the name of its class.

Among the given statements:

a) Overloaded constructors should be defined using different lists of parameters.
This statement is true. Overloaded constructors need to have different sets of parameters in order to differentiate them.

b) Overloaded constructors can only be defined by changing the access modifier.
This statement is false. Overloaded constructors can have the same access modifier or different access modifiers. Changing the access modifier is not mandatory for overloading constructors.

c) Overloaded constructors can have different access modifiers.
This statement is true. Overloaded constructors can have different access modifiers such as public, private, protected, or default (package-private).

d) A constructor can call another constructor using the keyword this.
This statement is true. A constructor can invoke another constructor within the same class using the keyword "this" along with appropriate arguments.

e) A constructor can call another constructor using the name of its class.
This statement is also true. A constructor can call another constructor within the same class using the name of the class, known as a constructor chaining. It is done using the "ClassName()" syntax.

In conclusion, statements a, c, d, and e are true, while statement b is false.
wrong answer

Apologies for the confusion. Let's analyze the statements again:

a) Overloaded constructors should be defined using different lists of parameters.
This statement is true. Overloaded constructors need to have different lists of parameters to distinguish them from each other.

b) Overloaded constructors can only be defined by changing the access modifier.
This statement is false. Overloaded constructors can have the same access modifiers. The number or types of parameters should be different to overload constructors.

c) Overloaded constructors can have different access modifiers.
This statement is true. Overloaded constructors can have different access modifiers. This applies to any method or constructor in Java.

d) A constructor can call another constructor using the keyword this.
This statement is true. A constructor can invoke another constructor using the "this" keyword. This is called constructor chaining and allows for code reuse.

e) A constructor can call another constructor using the name of its class.
This statement is false. In Java, constructors cannot call other constructors using the name of the class. The "this" keyword is used to call other constructors.

So, statements a, c, and d are true, while statements b and e are false.

Yes, the given code will be compiled without any errors. 

The code is defining a class named "Boo" with a static variable "i" and an instance initializer block. The instance initializer block is surrounded by curly braces {} and is executed when an instance of the class is created. 

In this case, the instance initializer block assigns the value 5 to the static variable "i". The code is valid and will compile successfully.

Yes, the given code will be compiled without any errors.

The code is defining a class named "Boo" with a final variable "b" and an instance initializer block. The instance initializer block is surrounded by curly braces {} and is executed when an instance of the class is created.

In this case, the instance initializer block is assigning the value 10 to the final variable "b". Since the variable "b" is declared as final, it must be assigned a value exactly once before the constructor completes. The assignment within the instance initializer block satisfies this requirement, so the code is valid and will compile successfully.

Task description
Define an immutable class Pair with two constant integer fields, a and b. This class must contain a private parameterized constructor.

Implement the following methods:

Pair newInstance(int a, int b) – creates a new instance of Pair, which will initialize its fields with the given arguments.
Pair swappedCopy() – returns a swapped copy in which fields a and b are swapped.
Pair orderedCopy() – returns an ordered copy in which a <= b.


Add the toString method, which should return a string representation of the Pair instance given in the predefined code.

Declare a package-private class Solution with the main method to check the functionality of the Pair class.

The input data elements are the integers a0 and b0, separated by a space.

Create a Pair instance with these values.

Output this instance and its swapped and ordered copies, separated by a space.


The functionality of the Pair class will be verified by the Main class on the Examinator side. See the code for the Main class below.
